chunk_summary: This section focuses on the micronutrient supplementation schedule for inpatient care of children with severe acute malnutrition (SAM), emphasizing infection management and metabolic corrections based on established guidelines and protocols.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of micronutrient supplementation in managing SAM in inpatient settings.
Learn the recommended schedule and dosages for micronutrient administration in SAM care.
Identify key metabolic corrections required during SAM treatment.
Explore infection management strategies in children with SAM.
key_facts: Micronutrient supplementation is critical for recovery in SAM and includes vitamins A, D, and zinc.
Vitamin A is administered at 50,000 IU for infants under 6 months, 100,000 IU for 6-12 months, and 200,000 IU for children over 12 months.
Iron supplementation is delayed until the child shows signs of recovery to avoid exacerbating infections.
Zinc supplementation is recommended at 2 mg/kg/day to support immune function and recovery.
SAM management includes addressing hypoglycemia and hypothermia as part of metabolic corrections.
Infection management involves empirical antibiotic therapy due to high risk of sepsis in SAM patients.
reference_quote: Vitamin A supplementation is essential in SAM care, with dosages varying by age: 50,000 IU for infants under 6 months, 100,000 IU for 6-12 months, and 200,000 IU for children over 12 months. (Source: WHO Pocket Book of Hospital Care for Children)
